refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall establish and maintain teleoperation communication channels with end-to-end latency (sensor-to-actuator round-trip) not exceeding 150 ms at the 95th percentile under agreed network conditions, enabling safe and responsive remote equipment operation.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR is vague on measurement points and statistical bounds. Refinement clarifies that latency is measured end-to-end (from sensor acquisition through network transmission to actuator command execution), specifies the p95 percentile metric, and anchors to the acceptance criterion. This enables testable verification and distinguishes latency management from other teleoperation concerns (e.g., bandwidth, reliability, security).
refined_fr.verification_idea: Instrument teleoperation path with timestamped events at sensor output, network ingress/egress, and actuator input. Collect latency samples across representative network conditions (agreed baseline, congestion, packet loss scenarios). Compute p95 latency distribution. Acceptance: p95 ≤ 150 ms.
sub_frs: FR-TELE-001: Acquire and timestamp sensor data at the equipment with minimal local processing delay
FR-TELE-002: Transmit sensor telemetry to tele-operator with bounded network latency
FR-TELE-003: Receive and decode operator commands at the equipment with bounded network latency
FR-TELE-004: Execute operator commands on actuators with minimal local processing delay
FR-TELE-005: Monitor and report teleoperation latency status to safety services for real-time compliance verification

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement dedicated low-latency teleoperation network stack with priority queuing, hardware timestamping, and optimized serialization; minimize local processing via edge compute at vehicle; use predictive buffering and jitter compensation at tele-operator console.

DP-B: Deploy hybrid architecture with local autonomy fallback; tele-operator sends high-level commands (not continuous control); vehicle executes with local closed-loop control; telemetry streamed asynchronously; latency requirement applies only to safety-critical command acknowledgment.

DP-C: Establish dedicated low-latency communication channel (separate from general telemetry) using hardware-accelerated network interface, real-time OS kernel scheduling, and deterministic packet routing; implement end-to-end latency monitoring with automatic fallback to safe state if latency exceeds threshold.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-C: Establish dedicated low-latency communication channel with hardware acceleration, real-time scheduling, deterministic routing, and active latency monitoring with safe-state fallback.

potential_sources: DP-C couples teleoperation latency to network infrastructure design (must support deterministic routing and QoS); couples to vehicle real-time OS and scheduler; couples to safety services for fallback logic.
Decoupling achieved by isolating the dedicated channel from general telemetry traffic and by making fallback logic a safety service responsibility rather than embedding it in teleoperation module.
coupling_check.notes: DP-C is preferred over DP-A (which adds complexity without determinism) and DP-B (which relaxes the latency requirement, conflicting with the acceptance criterion for continuous teleoperation). DP-C maintains independence of teleoperation logic from application-level command semantics.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Network infrastructure can be configured to support deterministic routing and QoS prioritization (agreed network conditions).
Assumption: Vehicle hardware includes real-time capable network interface and OS kernel.
Risk: If network conditions degrade beyond agreed baseline, latency may exceed 150 ms; mitigation via safety fallback.
Risk: Hardware timestamping accuracy and clock synchronization between vehicle and tele-operator; mitigation via calibration and redundant timing sources.
Risk: Fallback to safe state may interrupt operator control; mitigation via safety policy and operator training.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Clarify in DP-C the definition of 'safe state' and the trigger conditions for fallback (e.g., latency > 150 ms for > 500 ms duration). Specify the real-time OS and network interface requirements as design constraints to be flowed down to vehicle architecture.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: Owns the dedicated low-latency communication channel, including network stack optimization, hardware timestamping, real-time packet scheduling, latency monitoring, and interface to Safety Services for fallback triggering. Responsible for end-to-end latency measurement and reporting. Does not own vehicle actuator control logic, sensor acquisition hardware, or network infrastructure.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module outputs timestamped sensor telemetry (position, orientation, load, diagnostics) to tele-operator console via dedicated low-latency channel. Includes latency metadata.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module receives operator commands (movement, tool control, mode changes) from tele-operator console via dedicated low-latency channel. Commands are timestamped and validated before forwarding to vehicle control.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module reports latency status and compliance to Safety Services. Receives fallback directives (e.g., 'enter safe state') from Safety Services when latency exceeds threshold or other safety conditions are violated.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module interfaces with the network infrastructure to send/receive packets. Requires QoS configuration, deterministic routing, and hardware timestamping support.

module_containment_scores.notes: NGHE Solution is the system-level container but is too coarse-grained to own teleoperation latency logic. Teleoperation is a distinct subsystem with specialized real-time requirements. Assigning to NGHE Solution would diffuse responsibility and obscure ownership.

Vehicle owns actuator control and local sensor acquisition but does not own the tele-operator console or network transmission logic. Latency spans vehicle and remote console; assigning to Vehicle alone would split responsibility and obscure end-to-end latency ownership.

Teleoperation module is purpose-built for remote operation and naturally owns the dedicated communication channel, latency monitoring, and interface to safety services. Containment is high because the module boundary aligns with the functional scope (end-to-end latency management). Diffusion is minimal; no other module has competing responsibility.

Safety Services owns safety policy enforcement and fallback logic but does not own the teleoperation communication channel itself. Assigning latency management to Safety Services would conflate safety policy with communication infrastructure, reducing clarity.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Teleoperation module is the natural owner of CR-NGHE-008 because it encapsulates the dedicated low-latency communication channel, real-time scheduling, latency monitoring, and the interface to Safety Services. The module boundary aligns with the functional requirement (end-to-end latency) and avoids diffusion across vehicle, network, and safety domains.
containment_risks: Risk: Teleoperation module depends on Vehicle and Network Link modules for sensor data and network transport; if those modules introduce latency, Teleoperation cannot fully control end-to-end latency. Mitigation: Define latency budgets for each subsystem (sensor acquisition, network transmission, command execution) and allocate to owning modules.

Risk: Safety Services owns the fallback policy; if fallback logic is not tightly integrated with Teleoperation latency monitoring, there may be a gap in response time. Mitigation: Define a clear SafetyAlert interface with explicit latency thresholds and fallback triggers.

Risk: Network infrastructure (QoS, routing) is external to NGHE Solution; if network conditions degrade, Teleoperation cannot guarantee p95 ≤ 150 ms. Mitigation: Establish agreed network conditions as a constraint and define fallback behavior when conditions are violated.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Establish a latency budget allocation: (1) Sensor acquisition and timestamping at Vehicle: ≤ 20 ms, (2) Network transmission (vehicle to console): ≤ 60 ms, (3) Console processing and operator reaction: ≤ 40 ms, (4) Network transmission (console to vehicle): ≤ 60 ms, (5) Command execution at Vehicle: ≤ 20 ms. Total: ≤ 200 ms (with 50 ms margin for p95 variation). Assign each budget to the owning module (Vehicle, Network Link, Teleoperation, Safety Services) and verify via integration testing.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-C (dedicated low-latency channel with hardware acceleration, real-time scheduling, deterministic routing, and active monitoring) directly addresses the refined FR (end-to-end latency ≤ 150 ms p95). The DP is specific, measurable, and achievable. No coupling issues identified that would prevent implementation. The DP includes fallback logic to handle network degradation, aligning with safety criticality.

diffusion_risks: Teleoperation module depends on Vehicle (sensor data), Network Link (transport), and Safety Services (fallback policy). These dependencies are necessary and well-defined via interfaces (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert, Network Link). No diffusion of latency ownership across modules.

Risk: If Vehicle or Network Link modules introduce unexpected latency, Teleoperation cannot compensate. Mitigation: Establish latency budgets and SLAs for each module; monitor and report via SafetyAlert interface.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation module has high containment (0.95) for the latency management function. The module boundary is clear: it owns the dedicated communication channel, real-time scheduling, latency monitoring, and safety interface. Vehicle and Network Link are external dependencies with defined interfaces. No competing ownership or functional overlap.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification is feasible via instrumentation of the teleoperation path (sensor output, network ingress/egress, actuator input) with timestamped events. Latency samples can be collected under agreed network conditions and analyzed for p95 compliance. Fallback behavior can be tested by injecting latency and verifying safe-state transition.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation is clear: Teleoperation module owns end-to-end latency measurement and reporting. Vehicle owns sensor acquisition latency budget. Network Link owns transmission latency budget. Safety Services owns fallback policy and safe-state enforcement. Each module has a defined responsibility and interface.

recommended_revisions: Establish and document latency budget allocation across Vehicle, Network Link, Teleoperation, and Safety Services modules. Include p95 targets for each subsystem.
Define 'agreed network conditions' explicitly (e.g., bandwidth, packet loss, jitter ranges) and document as a constraint.
Specify the real-time OS and network interface requirements for Vehicle and Teleoperation modules.
Define the SafetyAlert interface protocol for latency threshold reporting and fallback triggering.
Plan integration testing to verify end-to-end latency under agreed conditions and under degraded conditions (to validate fallback behavior).

decision.justification: The refined FR is well-defined and the DP is sound, but the FR is not atomic; it decomposes into five sub-FRs (sensor acquisition, network transmission, command reception, actuator execution, and latency monitoring). Each sub-FR requires its own DP and IM assignment at the next level. Proceeding to the next decomposition level will clarify ownership, interfaces, and latency budgets across the system.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-TELE-001: Acquire and timestamp sensor data at the equipment with minimal local processing delay (≤ 20 ms)
FR-TELE-002: Transmit sensor telemetry to tele-operator with bounded network latency (≤ 60 ms)
FR-TELE-003: Receive and decode operator commands at the equipment with bounded network latency (≤ 60 ms)
FR-TELE-004: Execute operator commands on actuators with minimal local processing delay (≤ 20 ms)
FR-TELE-005: Monitor and report teleoperation latency status to safety services for real-time compliance verification (≤ 10 ms monitoring cycle)

assumptions: Network infrastructure can be configured to support deterministic routing and QoS prioritization (agreed network conditions).
Vehicle hardware includes real-time capable network interface and OS kernel.
Tele-operator console has sufficient processing power to handle real-time telemetry and command generation.
Safety Services module is available and can enforce fallback policies via SafetyAlert interface.
Latency measurement is feasible via hardware timestamping or synchronized software clocks.
open_risks: Network conditions may degrade beyond agreed baseline, causing latency to exceed 150 ms. Mitigation: Fallback to safe state; define safe state in safety policy.
Hardware timestamping accuracy and clock synchronization between vehicle and tele-operator. Mitigation: Calibration and redundant timing sources.
Fallback to safe state may interrupt operator control. Mitigation: Safety policy and operator training.
Latency budget allocation may be infeasible if any subsystem cannot meet its target. Mitigation: Conduct feasibility study during detailed design; revise budgets if needed.
evidence_needed: Network infrastructure specification and QoS configuration documentation.
Vehicle hardware specification (real-time OS, network interface, sensor/actuator latency).
Tele-operator console specification (processing power, display latency).
Safety policy document defining safe state and fallback triggers.
Integration test plan and results demonstrating p95 latency ≤ 150 ms under agreed conditions.
Latency budget allocation and verification for each subsystem (FR-TELE-001 through FR-TELE-005).
